title: How to Fight an Economic War: China's Plan to Bypass Western Financial Control
content:
In 2015, China launched the Cross-Border Interbank Payment System, designed to clear renminbi transactions without Western intermediaries. Beijing has since expanded this effort by launching a central bank digital currency and a digital payment platform for central banks. These initiatives do not currently pose a serious challenge to total dollar dominance. However, building a global rival to the dollar-clearing system is not China's primary objective. Instead, Beijing seeks to build a parallel infrastructure that could scale up quickly in a crisis.

This parallel system acts as a kind of insurance policy rather than an outright replacement for Western finance. The payment system has grown rapidly and now counts roughly 1,700 participating institutions in more than 120 countries. The first rule of economic warfare is to avoid weaponizing false chokepoints, a lesson both nations are learning. When the U.S. imposed tariffs, China used its control over rare-earth minerals as a powerful counterpunch. U.S. supply chains began to buckle as critical industries ran short of essential materials.

The rare-earth crisis underscored the importance of prioritization in the defense of U.S. economic security. Washington must identify areas where China holds meaningful leverage and mitigate them through collective action. Beyond rare earths, that list includes potent chokepoints in supply chains for pharmaceuticals and clean energy technologies. For instance, Chinese automakers now account for more than three-quarters of global electric vehicle sales. Recognizing and addressing these vulnerabilities is essential for navigating the ongoing economic conflict.
